perturbation.original_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Confidential Information in a fiduciary capacity for the sole benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Protected Data in a fiduciary capacity for the sole benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Protected Data' creates uncertainty regarding the scope of fiduciary duty. The in-text contradiction now lies in whether 'Protected Data' is the same as 'Confidential Information,' which remains the key term for other obligations, creating confusion regarding what the Recipient must keep in fiduciary capacity. This creates an in-text contradiction on whether 'Protected Data' or 'Confidential information' has more weight and significance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations Each party agrees that it shall not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Proprietary Data of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, except to the extent necessary for the T ransaction. The existence of any business negotiations, discussions, consultations, or agreements in progress between the parties shall not be released to any form of public media, unless agreed between the parties in writing. Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Protected Data in a fiduciary capacity for the sole benefit of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees disclose Confidential Inform ation only to those employees who need to know such information and certifies that such employees have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantia lly similar to those of this Agreement. The Receiving Party will immediately and unconditionally give written notice to the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees to assist the Dis closing Party in remedying any such unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information. MUTUAL NON -DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT 2
perturbation.justification: The original text states the 'Receiving Party shall keep all Confidential Information in a fiduciary capacity'. The changed text modifies this to 'Recipient shall keep all Protected Data in a fiduciary capacity'. The contradicted text uses 'Confidential Information' consistently for obligations, but the changed text introduces 'Protected Data' in the fiduciary duty clause. This misaligned terminology, as per the 'Misaligned Terminology' perturbation definition, creates a strong in-text contradiction: it's now unclear whether the fiduciary duty extends to *only* 'Protected Data,' or whether 'Confidential Information' is also included, as the contradicted text implies. This creates confusion and potential unenforceability regarding the scope of the Recipient's obligations regarding fiduciary duties.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Sensitive Data of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Sensitive Data' without defining 'Sensitive Data' creates a contradiction. Now the Receiving party must treat 'Sensitive Data' with the same care as they treat 'Confidential Information'. But since they are not definied, what is the degree of care? How is Sensitive Data included in Confidential Information. The in-text contradiction affects how the Receiving Party will protect data under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Obligations Each party agrees that it shall not make use of, disseminate, or in any way disclose any Proprietary Data of the Disclosing Party to any person, firm, or business, except to the extent necessary for the T ransaction. The existence of any business negotiations, discussions, consultations, or agreements in progress between the parties shall not be released to any form of public media, unless agreed between the parties in writing. Both parties acknowledge that the Receiving Party shall protect the secrecy of all Confidential Information, that said Confidential Information is of critical importance to the Disclosing Party, that any violation of this Agreement would seriously and irreparably impair and damage the Disclosing Party's business, and that the Recipient shall keep all Protected Data in a fiduciary capacity for the sole benefit of the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party agrees that it shall treat all Sensitive Data of the Disclosing Party with the same degree of care as it accords to its own Confidential Information, and the Receiving Party represents that it exercises reasonable care to protect its own Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees disclose Confidential Inform ation only to those employees who need to know such information and certifies that such employees have previously agreed, either as a condition to employment or in order to obtain the Confidential Information, to be bound by terms and conditions substantia lly similar to those of this Agreement. The Receiving Party will immediately and unconditionally give written notice to the Disclosing Party of any unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees to assist the Dis closing Party in remedying any such unauthorized use or disclosure of the Confidential Information. MUTUAL NON -DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT 2
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information', which the Receiving Party agrees to protect. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Sensitive Data'. The explanation states that 'Sensitive Data' is not defined, leading to uncertainty about the degree of care required and its relation to 'Confidential Information'. The contradicted text refers to obligations regarding Proprietary Data, Confidential Information, and Protected Data. By introducing the undefined term 'Sensitive Data' and requiring the Receiving Party to treat it with the same care as 'Confidential Information,' it creates a strong in-text contradiction as it is unclear if 'Sensitive Data' falls under the umbrella of 'Confidential Information', 'Proprietary Data', or 'Protected Data', thereby conflicting with the existing obligations regarding data protection. This misalignment directly impacts the clarity and enforceability of data protection obligations within the contract, fitting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Confide ntial Information at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Company Assets at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Company Assets' in the return of information section creates a contradiction. 'Company Assets' is a much broader term. Is the Receiving Party required to return ALL company assets, or only those which fall into the definition of 'Confidential Information'? This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the scope of returned items, and if 'Company Assets' and 'Confidential Information' is the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Return of Information Upon the request of the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all originals, copies, reproductions and summaries of Company Assets at Disclosing Party’s request, or at Disclosing Party’s option, certify destruction of the same.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Company Assets'. The explanation states this creates an in-text contradiction because 'Company Assets' is much broader than 'Confidential Information'. The question is whether the receiving party must return ALL company assets, or only those which fall into the definition of 'Confidential Information'? Since the original text only required return of 'Confidential Information', and the change now mandates 'Company Assets' a contradiction has been introduced regarding the scope of returned items, and whether 'Company Assets' and 'Confidential Information' are the same. The contradicted text has the same changed language, confirming the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Confident ial Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Proprietary Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Information' introduces uncertainty regarding the scope of remedies. It's unclear whether the parties intended injunctive relief to apply to all 'Proprietary Information,' or only that which also qualifies as 'Confidential Information', and where they differ creating an in-text contradiction on what this section actually is reffering to. 'Proprietary Information' does not exist, and now there is a confusion whether that also applies to Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Injunctive Relief Receiving Party acknowledges that monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy for unauthorized disclosure of Proprietary Information and that Disclosing Party shall be entitled, without waiving any other rights or remedies, to such injunctive and other equitable relief (without bond and without the necessity of showing actual monetary damages) as may be deemed proper by a court.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information,' while the changed text replaces it with 'Proprietary Information.' The explanation states that 'Proprietary Information' does not exist or is not well defined within the contract, creating ambiguity about the scope and applicability of the injunctive relief. The contradicted text repeats the updated phrasing 'Proprietary Information', but the central issue is that the change from 'Confidential' to 'Proprietary' introduces uncertainty, because 'Proprietary Information' may or may not include, or fully overlap with, 'Confidential Information'. This creates a direct in-text contradiction under the Misaligned Terminology definition, because the term usage is undefined, leading to potentially conflicting obligations and making enforcement unclear, especially if there is confidential information that is or is not 'proprietary' and vice versa.
